local_names.romanized_local: Aoliao Tediao Naixi (Chinese), Milkshake Istimewa Oreo (Malay), Orio Sirappu Milkshek (Tamil), Oreo Special Milkshake (Hindi)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific Oreo milkshake entry; closest defensible parent match is the generic milkshake food product class FOODON_03301737.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers flavoured milk-based beverages; no specific Oreo milkshake code exists. This is the closest defensible FoodEx2 parent anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code for Oreo milkshake; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 250 g (one cup or glass), typical for a milkshake or blended dessert drink served in a café or food court setting.

nutrient_source.title: Milkshake, chocolate — Singapore HPB Foodo Database; supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Milk shakes, thick, vanilla (NDB 01110) for vanilla milkshake analogue
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input per_100g values (energy 165 kcal, protein 3.97 g, fat 5.66 g, carbohydrate 25.6 g, sugar 21.2 g, sodium 75 mg, dietary fibre 0.1 g, potassium 198 mg, calcium 129 mg, magnesium 14 mg, iron 0.61 mg, cholesterol 15 mg) are sourced from HPB Singapore at confidence 0.95 and are preserved as-is. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for thick vanilla milkshake (NDB 01110), which reports approximately 3.5 g saturated fat per 100 g for a comparable milk-and-ice-cream blend; adjusted to 3.2 g to reflect partial Oreo cookie contribution. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1 (data not available in primary source).

gi_source.title: Milkshake, vanilla, GI = 61 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283; University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for vanilla milkshake.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for 'Oreo Special' milkshake. The closest approved GI source entry is vanilla milkshake (GI = 61, medium) from the University of Sydney GI Database and the International Tables of GI Values (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283). The Oreo Special is a blended milk, vanilla ice cream, and Oreo cookie drink; the vanilla milkshake GI of 61 is used as the analogous estimate. Oreo cookies have a higher GI (~71), but their proportion in the blend is modest; the blended GI is estimated conservatively at 61 (medium). Glycemic load per serving = GI/100 × carbohydrate_g_per_serving = 61/100 × 64.0 g (25.6 g/100g × 250 g serving) = 39.04, rounded to 39.4.

hypertension.key_factor: High refined sugar (21.2 g/100 g) with negligible dietary fibre (0.1 g/100 g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention and weight gain, elevating blood pressure risk; sodium at 75 mg/100 g (187.5 mg per 250 g serving) adds a moderate direct sodium load.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Oreo Special milkshake due to its very high sugar content (21.2 g/100 g), which promotes insulin-driven sodium retention and contributes to weight gain — both established drivers of elevated blood pressure per AHA 2018 dietary guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 21.2 g/100 g exceeds the 20 g/100 g diabetes hard gate; dietary fibre is only 0.1 g/100 g, providing no glycaemic modulation; a 250 g serving delivers approximately 53 g of sugar, causing a severe and rapid blood glucose spike.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Oreo Special milkshake is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes: a single 250 g serving delivers approximately 53 g of sugar with virtually no fibre (0.25 g), producing a severe glycaemic excursion that is incompatible with blood glucose management targets.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat estimated at 3.2 g/100 g (8.0 g per 250 g serving) from dairy and Oreo palm oil, combined with 21.2 g/100 g refined sugar, promotes LDL elevation, triglyceride rise, and endothelial dysfunction; cholesterol is 15 mg/100 g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should avoid Oreo Special milkshake: its combination of saturated fat (~3.2 g/100 g from dairy and palm oil in Oreo cookies) and very high refined sugar (21.2 g/100 g) raises LDL cholesterol and triglycerides, worsening cardiovascular risk per AHA/ACC 2019 dietary guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Oreo Special is a blended dessert milkshake made from a creamy combination of vanilla ice cream or vanilla-flavoured dairy, whole milk, and crushed Oreo sandwich cookies. The drink is typically served chilled or blended to a thick, smooth consistency with a sweet cookies-and-cream flavour profile and a rich, creamy texture. It is popular in Singapore cafés, food courts, and dessert stalls as a sweet indulgence. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 165 kcal, 3.97 g protein, 5.66 g fat (including approximately 3.2 g saturated fat from dairy and palm oil in Oreo cookies), and 25.6 g carbohydrate of which 21.2 g is sugar. Dietary fibre is negligible at 0.1 g/100 g. Calcium is notably high at 129 mg/100 g from the dairy base. The drink is ultra-processed and high in refined sugar, making it clinically unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. Its glycaemic index is estimated at 61 (medium) based on the vanilla milkshake analogue from the University of Sydney GI Database, but the glycaemic load per 250 g serving is high at approximately 39 g due to the large carbohydrate volume. It contains allergens including milk, wheat, soy, and gluten from the Oreo cookies.
common_user_queries: How many calories in an Oreo milkshake?
Is Oreo Special suitable for diabetics?
What is the sugar content of an Oreo shake?
Can I drink Oreo milkshake if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of an Oreo milkshake?
How much fat is in an Oreo Special drink?

confidence_reason: Core macros are sourced from HPB Singapore at 0.95 confidence and are preserved as-is. Saturated fat is estimated from the closest USDA SR Legacy analogue (thick vanilla milkshake, NDB 01110) and adjusted for Oreo cookie contribution. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database vanilla milkshake entry. Several micronutrient fields (monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, vitamins, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, water) are not available in the HPB source and are set to -1. Overall confidence is medium due to the adapted saturated fat value and analogous GI estimate.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, fat-soluble vitamins, B-vitamins, and moisture data for this specific blended Oreo milkshake preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this exact product as of 2026-04-16. 2. Saturated fat (3.2 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy thick vanilla milkshake (NDB 01110, approximately 3.5 g/100 g) adjusted downward to reflect the partial Oreo cookie contribution to the fat profile. 3. GI of 61 is an analogous estimate based on vanilla milkshake from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283); no direct GI measurement for Oreo Special milkshake exists in approved sources. 4. The input record classified this food under 'Grains and staples / Baking ingredients'; this has been corrected to 'Beverages / Milkshakes and blended dessert drinks' based on the food description ('a creamy blend of vanilla, oreo, milk') and nutritional profile. 5. Halal status is marked false as Oreo cookies' halal certification varies by market and cannot be confirmed without product-specific documentation. 6. No zero-macro justification is required as protein_g (3.97), fat_g (5.66), and carbohydrate_g (25.6) are all non-zero. 7. Ontology match types set to 'closest_parent' for both FoodOn and FoodEx2 as no exact code exists for Oreo milkshake in either ontology.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is 'Oreo Special Milkshake', a precise canonical English name. The input name 'Oreo Special' is preserved as the primary alias. Mandatory local-language names for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are present in local_names.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (165.0), protein_g (3.97), fat_g (5.66), carbohydrate_g (25.6) are all present, numeric, and sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo at 0.95 confidence. nutrient_source documents the HPB primary source and USDA SR Legacy adaptation for saturated fat.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.1), sodium_mg (75.0), saturated_fat_g (3.2), sugar_g (21.2) are all present as numeric values. Values are plausible and supported by HPB source (fibre, sodium, sugar) and USDA SR Legacy adaptation (saturated fat).

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (61), gi_category ('medium'), glycemic_load_per_serving (39.4), and gi_source are all present. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 Diabetes Care, which is an approved GI source. Estimation basis is fully documented in gi_source.source_match_notes.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn FOODON_03301737 (milkshake food product) is a defensible closest_parent match. FoodEx2 A0FKP (milk-based beverages, flavoured) is a defensible closest_parent match. Both foodon_match_type and foodex2_match_type are set to 'closest_parent', which is a valid value from the allowed set ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none']. SNOMED CT is null with honest gap note. No codes are invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters (approximately 1,050 characters). It explains what the food is, how it is prepared, taste/texture, cultural context (Singapore cafés/food courts), clinical relevance (T2D, hypertension, CVD), and key nutrients. common_user_queries, meal_context, and ingredient_synonyms are populated.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions are present: Hypertension (high concern), Type 2 Diabetes (high concern, human_condition_name correctly set to 'Type 2 Diabetes', machine key 'type2_diabetes'), Cardiovascular Disease (high concern). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and a specific clinical guidance sentence.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: Self-check performed: all strings in aliases_by_language (english: 7 entries, chinese: 2 entries, malay: 1 entry, tamil: 1 entry, hindi: 1 entry, romanized: 4 entries) appear verbatim in the flat aliases array. Flat aliases array contains 14 entries covering English variants, Chinese script forms, Malay, Tamil, Hindi, and romanized forms. No aliases_by_language entry is missing from the flat aliases array.
